svg.title: Section 1.4.4: what each paradigm hallucinates, and where it is caught
svg.desc: The imperative arm and the SDP arm hallucinate different things. The imperative agent's dominant hallucination is inventing nonexistent I/O paths, which nothing structural can catch, so it surfaces only at runtime (96 percent of its error iterations) and drives fail-loops and wasted compute. The SDP agent's hallucinations are imperative habits written into a declarative pipeline (setting session config, eager actions in a query function) plus invented undeclared tables and columns; 39 percent are caught at the cheap dry-run gate before any compute. Neither arm hallucinated Databricks DLT.
svg.text: What each paradigm hallucinates, and where it is caught

g.text: IMPERATIVE (A): invents where the data lives
caught only at RUNTIME (96% of error-iters)
un-gateable, agent loops re-guessing, wasted compute (1.4.3)
39% caught at the cheap DRY-RUN GATE, before compute
the same spark.conf.set line: legitimate in imperative, a gate-caught hallucination in SDP

svg.text: Databricks DLT hallucination (import dlt, @dlt): 0 in BOTH arms.
Imperative has no framework to confuse; the SDP arm's governed pyspark-sdp skill keeps it on the OSS API.
The gate catches a whole class of hallucination cheaply; imperative's dominant one is un-gateable.
§1.4.2's dry-run does not only catch structural defects (79 vs 0), it moves paradigm-confusion hallucinations from expensive-runtime to
cheap-structural. The arms are not more-vs-less hallucinating; they hallucinate different things.
Qualitative characterization over the exploratory sweeps (imperative 158 runs, SDP 145). Recompute: study/analysis/hallucination_taxonomy.py.
